audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/anodizer-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-j31y9_dt...

Analyzing anodizer-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-j31y9_dt) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard source URLs pointing to versioned release assets on GitHub (`https://github.com/tj-smith47/anodizer/releases/download/v${pkgver}/...`). These URLs are not obfuscated and point directly to tarballs matching the declared `pkgver`. The `sha256sums` for both architectures are provided and fixed, ensuring integrity of the downloaded binaries. No command substitution, dynamic fetching, or execution occurs in the global scope or within a `pkgver()` function — in fact, `pkgver` is hard-coded, so no network calls are triggered during sourcing.

The `source` arrays use proper syntax to rename the downloaded tarballs locally, but the actual URLs are from the project's official upstream. There are no `git+https` sources, no use of `eval`, `curl | bash`, or other risky constructs at the top level. The `package()` function is irrelevant for this narrow audit gate since it will not be executed with `--noprepare --nobuild --nodeps`. Simply sourcing the PKGBUILD and downloading the sources poses no risk of remote code execution or data exfiltration.

All operations during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` are limited to fetching the declared sources and parsing the PKGBUILD metadata, which is safe in this case.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code execution during source fetch and parse
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code execution during source fetch and parse[/]

Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/anodizer-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-epax1osg...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is straightforward and well-structured. It defines architecture-specific sources (`source_aarch64` and `source_x86_64`) pointing to GitHub release assets from the package's own upstream repository (`github.com/tj-smith47/anodizer`). Both sources include pinned `sha256sums` for integrity verification, which is good practice for binary packages.

There is no top-level command substitution, no `pkgver()` function, and no code that executes during sourcing beyond simple variable assignments. The `package()` function (which is out of scope for this gate) only performs standard install operations. Nothing in this PKGBUILD would execute malicious code during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`.
</details>
<summary>Standard binary package fetching upstream GitHub releases with checksums.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard binary package fetching upstream GitHub releases with checksums.[/]

Audit Skips:

[SKIPPED] makepkg --nobuild failed, unable to download sources.

data.reasoning: Both models only audited the top-level PKGBUILD as a safety gate before running makepkg, then skipped the full audit because `makepkg --nobuild` failed to download sources. No files beyond the PKGBUILD were reviewed. The PKGBUILD itself was assessed as SAFE by both models: it uses versioned, pinned GitHub release URLs with fixed SHA256 checksums, no pkgver() function, no command substitution, no eval/curl|bash patterns. This is a typical -bin binary package layout. However, because the actual binary tarball and any .install hooks could not be downloaded/reviewed, the package() function and potential install scripts were never inspected. The PKGBUILD analysis is reassuring but the audit is incomplete — hence inconclusive rather than safe.
data.disagreement_analysis: There is no substantive disagreement. Both models reached identical conclusions (PKGBUILD safe at the gate level, full audit skipped due to source download failure). The review was triggered as 'shallow' simply because 0 files were reviewed after the gate.
coverage_issues: package() function was never reviewed for what it installs and where
Any .install hook (post_install/post_upgrade) was not retrieved or audited
The actual downloaded binary blob could not be inspected (though binary inspection is generally out of scope/impractical)
Source download failure prevented review of all non-PKGBUILD files
re_audit_focus: Resolve the source download failure (possibly a transient network/asset URL issue) and re-run with full source availability
package() function contents and install destinations
Presence and contents of any .install script
learnings: A failed `makepkg --nobuild` source download is an infrastructure/transient issue, not a security signal — the tool should distinguish 'skipped due to download failure' from 'reviewed and clean' and ideally retry with backoff or fall back to fetching the .install and reviewing PKGBUILD's package() statically.

Even when sources cannot be downloaded, the audit could still statically review the package() function and any committed .install file from the AUR git checkout, since these are present in the repo regardless of binary download success.

For -bin packages, the highest-value targets after PKGBUILD are .install hooks and the package() install logic; the tool should prioritize fetching/reviewing these independently of the full source tarball.
Both models correctly scoped the gate-level PKGBUILD assessment and avoided false positives, indicating the gate prompt is working well; the gap is purely coverage caused by the download failure.
